title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who joined neighbourhood emergency-preparedness teams. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Ruth, The Flood Warden
Ruth volunteered as a flood warden after water entered her street twice in one winter and the council appealed for local volunteers. [47]Her first task was mapping which elderly residents needed help moving furniture upstairs before warnings became urgent, because lifts often stopped working.[/47] She expected sandbags to be the main issue, but phone numbers and spare keys mattered more, especially for people living alone. Ruth had managed a hotel reception, and [48]that experience helped her keep calm when several frightened callers rang at the same time during a storm.[/48] She also learned to avoid dramatic language in messages, because panic spread faster than water. The most frustrating part was persuading people to prepare before the sky looked threatening. Ruth says she now respects quiet early preparation more than heroic rescues on camera, especially after seeing how a five-minute checklist prevented one neighbour losing important documents, photographs and prescription papers.
texts.B:
### Samir, The First-Aid Trainer
Samir originally joined the local emergency group to teach basic first aid in the community centre after weekend markets had closed. [49]He changed his sessions after realising that many adults were embarrassed to practise chest compressions in front of neighbours.[/49] He began using smaller groups and allowed people to rehearse on cushions before using the training manikin in the middle of the room. As a former schoolteacher, he knew how to turn nervous laughter into concentration without making anyone feel foolish, a skill he had learned with teenagers. [50]The question he heard most often was not technical, but whether helping someone could accidentally make things worse.[/50] Samir answered by focusing on simple actions that were safer than doing nothing while waiting for an ambulance. He says success came when a shop assistant later used the recovery position and then returned to class to describe what had happened in the aisle.
texts.C:
### Jade, The Radio Contact
Jade usually operated the volunteer emergency radio during long power cuts, passing messages between streets when mobile networks failed during storms or long power cuts. [51]She had to memorise call signs because writing them down became impossible in heavy persistent rain.[/51] At first, the clipped style of radio speech felt rude to her, but she came to appreciate its efficiency under pressure. Jade worked in a bakery, so early starts did not bother her, and she often tested equipment before sunrise on the hill above the estate. [52]Her worst mistake was forgetting to charge the spare battery, which left one team unreachable for twenty minutes.[/52] After that embarrassing evening, she taped a charging checklist inside the radio box beside the microphone. Jade says the role taught her that communication is not about saying more; it is about saying the right thing clearly enough for others to act quickly and safely.
texts.D:
### Victor, The Shelter Organiser
Victor helped set up a temporary shelter in the sports hall during severe winter weather, usually with very little warning. [53]He discovered that placing families near the exit made them anxious, so he rearranged the mats to create a quieter corner.[/53] He had originally expected blankets and hot drinks alone to solve most problems, but privacy proved just as important. [54]His logistics background helped him label boxes so volunteers could find nappies, torches and pet food without asking him first.[/54] The most touching moment was a teenager quietly charging everyone's phones from a power bank he had brought for himself. Victor also quickly learned that some people refused help if forms looked too official, so he simplified the sign-in sheet to one calm page. [55]He now insists that every drill includes a lost-child scenario.[/55] [56]He believes a shelter is successful when people leave feeling remembered, not processed by a system.[/56]

questions.question: started by identifying residents who would need help before the situation became critical?
questions.explanation: Ruth mapped elderly residents who needed help moving furniture before warnings were urgent.

questions.question: used experience dealing with guests to stay composed during many urgent calls?
questions.explanation: Ruth's hotel reception experience helped her remain calm when several callers rang during a storm.

questions.question: altered training because participants felt self-conscious practising in public?
questions.explanation: Samir changed sessions when adults were embarrassed to practise compressions in front of neighbours.

questions.question: says people's main concern was whether intervention might cause harm?
questions.explanation: Samir often heard people ask whether helping could accidentally make things worse.

questions.question: needed to remember identifying codes because weather prevented note-taking?
questions.explanation: Jade memorised call signs because writing them down was impossible in heavy rain.

questions.question: admits that an overlooked power supply problem temporarily cut off contact?
questions.explanation: Jade forgot to charge a spare battery, leaving one team unreachable.

questions.question: changed the layout after noticing where people felt uneasy?
questions.explanation: Victor rearranged mats after finding families near the exit became anxious.

questions.question: used organisational skills to make supplies easier for others to locate?
questions.explanation: Victor labelled boxes so volunteers could find supplies without asking him.

questions.question: argues that practice exercises should include a missing child situation?
questions.explanation: Victor insists every drill includes a lost-child scenario.

questions.question: defines success as making people feel personally acknowledged?
questions.explanation: Victor believes a shelter succeeds when people leave feeling remembered.
